bytes.topic: IAM Fundamentals — identity vs principal, authentication vs authorization
bytes.title: Vault — Identity vs Principal, Authentication vs Authorization
bytes.body: In Vault, identities and principals are often used interchangeably but differ in scope. An identity represents a user, group, or service account, while a principal is an entity allowed to perform actions within a specific namespace. Authentication ensures the identity of a user or service, whereas authorization determines their access to sensitive data. When authenticating with Vault, users are mapped to identities and principals. For example, a user can be authenticated as an engineer and assigned to a principal with read-only access to a namespace.

bytes.topic: Least Privilege — start with no permissions, grant what's needed, regular access review
bytes.title: Vault - Least Privilege Principle: Grant Only What's Needed
bytes.body: Least privilege is a security best practice where users and systems are granted only the permissions necessary to perform their tasks. In Vault, this is achieved by creating dynamic secrets engines with tightly controlled access. Start by creating a secrets engine with no policies or roles assigned, then incrementally add policies and roles as needed. Regularly review access to ensure it remains least privilege.

bytes.topic: IAM Policies — allow vs deny, condition keys, policy evaluation logic
bytes.title: Vault IAM Policies — Allow vs Deny, Condition Keys, and Policy Evaluation Logic
bytes.body: Vault IAM policies use a unique logic to evaluate access. Unlike traditional allow/deny models, Vault uses a logical AND operation to combine rules. Condition keys play a crucial role in policy evaluation. A condition key is a key-value pair that can be used to grant or deny access. When evaluating a policy, Vault checks if the condition key exists and matches the specified value. If the condition key is missing or does not match, the policy is denied. The policy evaluation logic also considers the `path` and `capabilities` fields. The `path` field specifies the resource or API endpoint being accessed, while the `capabilities` field specifies the actions that can be performed on that resource.

bytes.topic: Zero Trust Architecture — never trust, always verify, microsegmentation
bytes.title: Vault Zero Trust Architecture: Segregating Secrets with Dynamic Group Membership
bytes.body: In a Zero Trust Architecture, access is granted based on least privilege and dynamic conditions. Vault achieves this through dynamic group membership, which allows you to assign policies to groups that are determined at runtime. You can use the `group` entity to create groups and the `dynamic_group` entity to create dynamically assigned groups based on attributes and conditions. To integrate with your existing infrastructure, Vault provides a `transformer` function that enables you to fetch data from external systems and use it to update groups.

bytes.topic: WAF — rule sets, rate limiting, bot protection, tuning false positives
bytes.title: Fine-Tuning Vault's Web Application Firewall (WAF) Rules for Better Security
bytes.body:
Vault's WAF is enabled by default. To optimize performance, you can create custom rule sets using the `vault secrets engines auth` API. Define rate limiting and bot protection rules using the `rate` and `ip` blocks, respectively. To reduce false positives, use the `regex` and `transform` blocks to refine rules. For example, to block traffic from a specific IP address, create a rule with the `deny` action and the IP address in the `ip` block: 
```bash
vault secrets engines auth 
  create or update 
    - name='waf' 
    - rule='deny
      ip=192.168.1.1'
```
This code creates a WAF rule that denies traffic from the IP address 192.168.1.1.

bytes.topic: Service Accounts & Roles — machine identities, workload identity federation, no long-lived keys
bytes.title: Vault - Service Accounts & Roles: No Long-Lived Keys
bytes.body: Vault supports service accounts and roles for secure authentication and authorization. When creating a service account, you can assign roles to it using the `vault auth service-account create` command. This command takes the service account name, the role name, and the role ID as arguments. You can also use the `vault auth service-account update` command to update the role assignments for a service account. To list all roles assigned to a service account, use the `vault auth service-account read` command with the `-output` option set to `json`. This will return a JSON object containing the service account details, including the assigned roles.

bytes.topic: Encryption — KMS, customer-managed keys, envelope encryption
bytes.title: Vault - KMS, Customer-Managed Keys, and Envelope Encryption
bytes.body: Vault supports two types of encryption: symmetric and asymmetric. Symmetric encryption uses a single key, while asymmetric encryption uses a key pair. Vault's KMS (Key Management Service) allows you to store and manage encryption keys. Customer-managed keys are stored and managed outside of Vault. Envelope encryption combines symmetric encryption for performance and asymmetric encryption for key management. Use `vault write secret/secret/` to create a new secret and `vault kv put` to store encrypted data. Use `vault read` to retrieve encrypted data.

bytes.topic: Network Security — security groups, NACLs, private subnets, bastion hosts
bytes.title: Vault Cloud Security — Securing Your AWS Resources with Vault
bytes.body: When securing your AWS resources with Vault, it's essential to use network security groups, NACLs, and private subnets to control access. Use Vault's AWS provider to create and manage these resources. For example, you can use the `vault aws iam get-user` command to retrieve the IAM user credentials, and then use a network security group to restrict access to your EC2 instance. Additionally, use a bastion host to access your private subnet securely.

bytes.topic: Cloud Security Posture — CSPM tools, detecting misconfigurations at scale
bytes.title: Vault: Detecting Misconfigurations at Scale with Vault's Built-in CSPM
bytes.body: To ensure cloud security posture, Vault's built-in Secret Engine for AWS IAM roles enables you to manage IAM permissions programmatically. However, misconfigurations can occur when creating or updating IAM roles. Vault's AWS IAM Secret Engine provides a `read_role_config` API to inspect IAM role configurations. Use the `config_path` parameter to specify the IAM role ARN. For instance, to detect misconfigurations in an IAM role with ARN `arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/vault-role`, use the following API call: `vault.read_role_config(arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/vault-role, config_path="/aws/iam/roles/vault-role")`. Vault will return the role configuration, allowing you to identify and fix misconfigurations.
bytes.code_snippet:
import vault

def detect_misconfigurations(role_arn: str, config_path: str):
    response = vault.read_role_config(role_arn, config_path)
    # Inspect response and fix misconfigurations
    return response

bytes.topic: Audit Logging — CloudTrail/Cloud Audit Logs, what to capture, retention
bytes.title: Vault Audit Logging - Best Practices for Cloud Security with Vault
bytes.body: Vault's audit logging capabilities mirror AWS CloudTrail and Azure Cloud Audit Logs, but with a unique set of requirements. To ensure secure cloud operations, capture the following events: API calls, secret access, and policy management changes. Set the minimum retention period to 24 hours for audit logs to comply with regulatory standards. To configure audit logging in Vault, use the `audit_log_config` API endpoint, specifying the desired retention period and storage backend.

bytes.topic: Incident Response — isolation playbooks, forensics, blast radius containment
bytes.title: Vault Incident Response: Containing Blast Radius with Isolation Playbooks
bytes.body:
When an incident occurs in Vault, isolating the affected system and containing the blast radius are crucial steps in incident response. Vault provides a built-in feature called Isolation Playbooks, which allows you to automate this process. To create an Isolation Playbook, use the `vault write` command with the `path` parameter set to `sys/iso/playbook`. For example:

```bash
vault write sys/iso/playbook \
  name=my-playbook \
  command=\"vault lease revoke -prefix=my-namespace\\"

bytes.topic: Container Security — pod security standards, runtime security with Falco
bytes.title: Vault Integrates with Falco for Runtime Security
bytes.body: Vault's Secret Engine integrates with Falco, an open-source runtime security project, to provide runtime security for containers. To enable Falco integration, configure the Falco Secret Engine in your Vault instance using the `vault secrets enable falco` command. Once enabled, Vault provides Falco with the necessary secrets and configuration to detect and prevent security threats in your containers. Use the `vault read falco/config` command to retrieve the Falco configuration.
bytes.code_snippet:
vault secrets enable falco
vault write falco/config --config='{"output": "stdout"}'

bytes.topic: HashiCorp Vault — secret engines, dynamic secrets, lease TTL, auth methods
Secrets Rotation — automated rotation, zero-downtime rotation, audit trails
Federated Identity — SSO, SAML, OIDC, cross-account access patterns
bytes.title: Vault Federated Identity — Secure Cross-Account Access with SAML, OIDC, and SSO
bytes.body: To enable secure cross-account access in Vault, create a SAML or OIDC authentication method and associate it with an entity. Use the `auth/entity/login` endpoint to authenticate users from the external identity provider. Once authenticated, Vault creates a new entity-based login token. Use the `login/token/lookup` endpoint to retrieve the login token and access the desired secrets. For SSO, configure the `sso` parameter in the `auth/entity/login` endpoint to enable single sign-on. Set `sso` to `true` to allow users to authenticate without a password.

bytes.topic: Data Classification — tagging sensitive data, enforcing access based on classification
bytes.title: Vault Data Classification — Tagging Sensitive Data with Custom Attributes
bytes.body: Vault allows you to classify sensitive data using custom attributes. You can tag a secret with a specific classification, such as 'pii' for personally identifiable information. To enforce access based on classification, you can create a dynamic secret engine using Vault's built-in `sys/mounts` API. This engine can check the classification of a secret and deny access if it's not within the allowed classifications. For example, you can use the `path` parameter to specify the allowed classifications.

bytes.topic: Security Benchmarks — CIS benchmarks, cloud provider security best practices
bytes.title: Vault Security - Enforcing CIS Benchmarks and Cloud Provider Best Practices
bytes.body: Vault provides a built-in feature to enforce CIS benchmarks and cloud provider security best practices. To enable this feature, configure the `cis_benchmark` and `cloud_provider` options in the `vault` configuration file. For example, to enable AWS CIS benchmark, set `cloud_provider` to `aws` and `cis_benchmark` to `aws_cis_1_2_0`. Then, use the `vault write` command to update the `cis_benchmark` configuration. You can also use the `vault read` command to verify the CIS benchmark configuration.

bytes.topic: Compliance as Code — policy as code with OPA, cloud guardrails
HashiCorp Vault — secret engines, dynamic secrets, lease TTL, auth methods
Secrets Rotation — automated rotation, zero-downtime rotation, audit trails
bytes.title: Vault Secrets Rotation — Automated, Zero-Downtime, and Audit Trails
bytes.body: Vault supports automated secrets rotation using the `update` method. To rotate a secret, update its version with the `update` API: `vault write /path/to/secret/rotate version=2`. For zero-downtime rotation, use the `lease_id` parameter: `vault write /path/to/secret/rotate lease_id=<lease_id>`. Vault provides an audit trail for all rotation operations, storing them in the `audit_log` backend. To access the audit trail, query the `audit_log` API: `vault read /sys/audit-log`. Use the `limit` parameter to retrieve a specific number of entries: `vault read /sys/audit-log?limit=10`.

bytes.topic: Compliance as Code — policy as code with OPA, cloud guardrails
HashiCorp Vault — secret engines, dynamic secrets, lease TTL, auth methods
bytes.title: Vault - Mastering Secret Engines and Dynamic Secrets
bytes.body: Vault uses secret engines to manage secrets, which can be AWS, Kubernetes, or generic. Dynamic secrets are generated on-the-fly by a secret engine, reducing the risk of secret exposure. Lease TTL (time-to-live) determines how long a secret is valid. For example, setting a low TTL for database credentials ensures they expire frequently. Auth methods authenticate users before they can access Vault. You can use the `auth-userpass` method to authenticate users with a username and password.

bytes.topic: Compliance as Code — policy as code with OPA, cloud guardrails
